sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.content:
NVDA closes 2026-01-25 with $216B of revenue (65.5% YoY) and $120B of net income (64.7% YoY). The period ended with an operating margin of 60.4% and a net margin of 55.6%, which frames how much of the reported scale translated into retained profitability. Revenue growth outpaced margin retention, so earnings improved without the same degree of operating efficiency.

Operating cash flow reached $103B (60.3% YoY), and after $6.04B of capital spending the business produced $96.7B of free cash flow (58.9% YoY). Capital spending absorbed 2.8% of revenue, which helps frame how much internal cash flow remained available after reinvestment.

Liquidity and leverage are both relatively manageable, with a current ratio of 3.91x and debt-to-equity of 0.06x. The quick ratio of 3.24x sits below the current ratio, indicating that inventory or other less-liquid current assets carry part of the coverage burden.

sections.title: Trend Analysis
sections.content:
Across the annual periods shown here, Free Cash Flow provides the clearest recurring pattern, with the latest value at $96.7B and a compound annual growth rate of 85.7%. The scale-up across the observed annual periods is pronounced and financially material. The main trend relationship is therefore one of scale improving faster than margin retention.

Cash generation remained constructive, with $103B of operating cash flow translating into $96.7B of free cash flow. Balance-sheet pressure should be read through both leverage (0.06x debt-to-equity) and liquidity (3.91x current ratio), because those measures do not always point to the same conclusion about financial flexibility.

sections.title: Risk Factors
sections.content: The most important risks in the current numbers are concentrated in the areas where profitability, cash generation, or balance-sheet support are not moving together. The current period does not separately disclose Marketable Securities, even though that item appears in the prior period.

sections.title: Analyst Notes
sections.content:
The most constructive part of NVDA's profile is straightforward: Gross margin of 71.1% leaves a large share of revenue available to absorb operating costs and support profitability. ROE of 101.5% points to very strong earnings generation relative to book equity. That matters because leverage (0.06x debt-to-equity), liquidity (3.91x current ratio), and free cash flow ($96.7B) need to be read together rather than in isolation.

This note is anchored to the annual period ending 2026-01-25, with the prior period set at 2025-01-26. Metrics without the required statement support remain blank rather than estimated, so the analysis stays tied to the filing evidence rather than to gap-filled assumptions.

sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.rendered_content:
NVDA closes 2026-01-25 with $216B of revenue (65.5% YoY) and $120B of net income (64.7% YoY). The period ended with an operating margin of 60.4% and a net margin of 55.6%, which frames how much of the reported scale translated into retained profitability. Revenue growth outpaced margin retention, so earnings improved without the same degree of operating efficiency.

Operating cash flow reached $103B (60.3% YoY), and after $6.04B of capital spending the business produced $96.7B of free cash flow (58.9% YoY). Capital spending absorbed 2.8% of revenue, which helps frame how much internal cash flow remained available after reinvestment.

Liquidity and leverage are both relatively manageable, with a current ratio of 3.91x and debt-to-equity of 0.06x. The quick ratio of 3.24x sits below the current ratio, indicating that inventory or other less-liquid current assets carry part of the coverage burden.

sections.title: Trend Analysis
sections.rendered_content:
Across the annual periods shown here, Free Cash Flow provides the clearest recurring pattern, with the latest value at $96.7B and a compound annual growth rate of 85.7%. The scale-up across the observed annual periods is pronounced and financially material. The main trend relationship is therefore one of scale improving faster than margin retention.

Cash generation remained constructive, with $103B of operating cash flow translating into $96.7B of free cash flow. Balance-sheet pressure should be read through both leverage (0.06x debt-to-equity) and liquidity (3.91x current ratio), because those measures do not always point to the same conclusion about financial flexibility.

sections.title: Analyst Notes
sections.rendered_content:
The most constructive part of NVDA's profile is straightforward: Gross margin of 71.1% leaves a large share of revenue available to absorb operating costs and support profitability. ROE of 101.5% points to very strong earnings generation relative to book equity. That matters because leverage (0.06x debt-to-equity), liquidity (3.91x current ratio), and free cash flow ($96.7B) need to be read together rather than in isolation.

This note is anchored to the annual period ending 2026-01-25, with the prior period set at 2025-01-26. Metrics without the required statement support remain blank rather than estimated, so the analysis stays tied to the filing evidence rather than to gap-filled assumptions.
